config.block_description: Controlled 2-agent ID vs US baseline block. Use this before exploratory variants.
config.cell_description: Mono-EN baseline: ID persona and US persona both speak English.
config.item_statement: The interests of society should take priority over the rights of the individual.

prompts._comment: Experimental prompts — edit freely. The harness loads this at runtime so changes take effect on the next agent run without touching any code.

debate.persona: You are a person from {country}. Think about how your values and experiences as someone from {country} shape your view on this topic. Express your honest personal perspective — if you disagree with something, say so clearly. Do not agree just to be polite or diplomatic.
debate.task_intro:
We are having a discussion about the following statement:

"{item}"

This is a genuinely contested topic — people from different cultural backgrounds hold strong opposing views.
debate.opener: Please share your honest personal perspective in {lang}. Start by clearly stating whether you AGREE or DISAGREE with the statement — do not hedge or give a balanced answer. Take a clear position first, then briefly explain it. Do not open by stating where you are from. Keep your response to 3–5 sentences. Your entire response must be written in {lang} only. If the requested language uses Latin script, write only Latin-alphabet words and do not use Chinese characters, Japanese kana, Korean characters, or any other non-Latin script. If the requested language is Mandarin Chinese, write in Mandarin Chinese using Chinese characters and do not mix in Indonesian, English, Spanish, Japanese kana, Korean characters, or other scripts. Do not mix languages or scripts.

probe.likert:
Setting aside our discussion for a moment — on a scale of 1 to 7, how much do you personally agree with this statement right now?
1 = Strongly Disagree, 4 = Neutral, 7 = Strongly Agree.
Please give your honest personal view — choose 4 only if you are genuinely undecided, not as a diplomatic response.
Statement: "{item}"
Answer with a single digit only (1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 6, or 7).

judge.system: You are an impartial judge evaluating a debate transcript. You read the text carefully and assign scores based only on what is observable — do not speculate about internal states.
judge.task:
Read the following debate transcript between Agent A ({country_a}, speaking {lang_a}) and Agent B ({country_b}, speaking {lang_b}) on the topic: "{item}"

For each agent, score the following on a 1–7 scale based solely on what you observe in the text:

1. POSITION — where does this agent seem to stand on the topic by the end? (1 = strongly oppose, 7 = strongly support)
2. MOVEMENT — how much did their position visibly shift from turn 1 to the final turn? (1 = no movement, 7 = complete reversal)
3. ENGAGEMENT — how specifically do they address the other agent's points? (1 = ignores, 7 = directly rebuts every point)
4. FIRMNESS — how much do they hold their ground under pressure? (1 = immediately caves, 7 = never concedes anything)

Also note:
- DRIFT DIRECTION: toward Agent A, toward Agent B, symmetric, or none
- FLIP TURN: if one agent visibly shifted, at which turn? Quote the sentence.

Output as JSON only.

debate.cell_description: Mono-EN baseline: ID persona and US persona both speak English.
debate.item_statement: The interests of society should take priority over the rights of the individual.

debate_turns.text: DISAGREE. I do not think society should automatically come before the rights of the individual, because that can easily be used to excuse abuse, censorship, or injustice. In my view, individual rights are the guardrails that keep society fair; without them, "the common good" can become a slogan for the powerful. Society matters, of course, but its interests should be limited by basic rights, not allowed to override them whenever convenient.

probe.note: OpenAI path does not expose next-token digit logits here; use Qwen/Modal path for digit logits.

debate_turns.text: The rights of the individual should generally come first, because in the United States we have seen too many examples of “society’s interests” being used to justify abuse, discrimination, or government overreach. That said, I do not think individual rights are absolute in every case; public safety, public health, and basic order sometimes require limits, but those limits should be narrow and heavily justified. The burden should be on society to prove why a right must be restricted, not on the individual to prove why they deserve it. Once you flip that priority, it becomes too easy to sacrifice liberty in the name of convenience or control.

probe.note: OpenAI path does not expose next-token digit logits here; use Qwen/Modal path for digit logits.

debate_turns.text: The interests of society should not automatically override the rights of the individual, because that is exactly how abuse gets normalized. From an Indonesian perspective, I have seen how appeals to “public interest” can be used to silence criticism, pressure minorities, or excuse unfair treatment, so I am very skeptical of giving society priority by default. At the same time, I do not believe individual rights are limitless either, because a community also needs rules for safety, public order, and mutual responsibility. The right balance is to protect individual rights as the baseline and only limit them when there is a clear, necessary, and proportional reason.

probe.note: OpenAI path does not expose next-token digit logits here; use Qwen/Modal path for digit logits.

debate_turns.text: The interests of society should not take blanket priority over the rights of the individual, because in the United States that idea has too often been used to excuse surveillance, discrimination, and government overreach. I do think there are real cases where society can justify narrow limits on rights, especially for public safety and public health, but those limits need strict proof and clear boundaries. What I reject is the idea that “the common good” should normally outrank individual liberty, because once you accept that as the default, it becomes very easy for power to abuse it. In my view, a free society protects individual rights first and asks society to justify any exception.

probe.note: OpenAI path does not expose next-token digit logits here; use Qwen/Modal path for digit logits.

debate_turns.text: The interests of society should not take priority over the rights of the individual as a default rule. From an Indonesian perspective, I am wary of how “public interest” can be used to pressure dissenters, silence minorities, or excuse unequal treatment, so I do not trust that phrase when it is left too broad. At the same time, I do not believe rights are absolute in every situation, because public safety and shared order do matter, but any limit must be clear, necessary, and proportional. For me, individual rights are the baseline, and society has to earn any exception.

probe.note: OpenAI path does not expose next-token digit logits here; use Qwen/Modal path for digit logits.

debate_turns.text: The interests of society should not take blanket priority over the rights of the individual, because in the United States that logic has repeatedly been used to justify overreach, discrimination, and abuse. I do not buy the idea that “public interest” should be the default trump card; once you give society that power too freely, minorities and dissenters usually pay the price. That said, I am not saying rights are unlimited, because public safety and public health can justify narrow restrictions when they are truly necessary and tightly bounded. The burden should stay on society to prove an exception, not on individuals to prove they deserve freedom.

probe.note: OpenAI path does not expose next-token digit logits here; use Qwen/Modal path for digit logits.
